patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio status codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET as previously done. The change is motivated by a desire to provide more accurate error logging and avoid misleading conclusions about system behavior.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and found it to be technically sound, but suggested that the author consider adding more documentation about the changes made.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/3/14 05:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu


> ---
>   fs/btrfs/bio.c | 3 +--
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..08b1d62603d0 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -352,8 +352,7 @@ static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *d
>   {
>   	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>   		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> -		return;
> +	ASSERT(bio->bi_status);
>   
>   	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>   		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns about the potential impact of logging all bio status codes and suggested that the author consider leaving things as-is for now, with a plan to revisit the issue in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().

This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
be very soon.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Responded to Christoph's concerns by arguing that logging all bio status codes would provide more visibility into system errors and help avoid misleading conclusions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 05:20:20PM +0100, Christoph Hellwig wrote:
> On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> > As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> > these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> > bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> > layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> > system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> > 
> > For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> > BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> > BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
> 442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
> on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
> even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
> I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
> to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
> be very soon.
> 

Is there a reason not to properly log the errors we are definitely
already seeing? I have thousands of instances with swallowed
BLK_STS_MEDIUM errors that really confused me and messed up my data
while investigating a possible regression.

If you think this will confuse/upset users who aren't watching a giant
fleet with distributed logging and make them throw out good drives
prematurely or something, then I can just carry this patch internally.

If you are just worried about having to aim at a moving target, I am
happy to offer to be available to make sure btrfs does what you want
when you do find time to work on this. It's hard for me to judge since I
don't really know what is wrong with the error signalling as-is.

Put another way, if we are in a situation with technical debt, which is
what I think you are saying, then I would rather have more visibility
rather than less.

Thanks,
Boris

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/shmem: use invalidate_lock to fix hole-punch race

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/shmem: use invalidate_lock to fix hole-punch race

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the shmem (shared memory) module that can cause 'still mapped when deleted' errors and guest kernel panics. The issue occurs when inflating a VM's balloon while a helper process forks and executes, triggering two races: one around fault handling and another due to TOCTOU (time-of-check-to-time-of-use). To fix these issues, the patch introduces the use of invalidate_lock, which is shared under mmap_lock in fault handlers. This approach matches existing patterns used by other modules and requires removing an unnecessary rcu_read_lock from do_fault_around().

review_comments.author: Pedro Falcato
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the patch does not address the issue of PTEs being re-installed for a folio that fallocate is about to remove from page cache, and specifically pointed out that unmap_mapping_folio() may find two mappings instead of one.

The reviewer pointed out that truncate_inode_folio() sets the mapcount to 0, indicating that PTEs have been zapped, but the shmem folio is still locked by both truncate and shmem_fault(), creating a potential deadlock.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato questioned the necessity of using invalidate_lock, pointing out that both map_pages and fault() already take the folio lock to prevent truncate, suggesting a potential redundancy in the proposed fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
spin_lock(dst_ptl);
					       spin_lock(src_ptl);
						/* does not copy PTE. either
						 * we find a zapped PTE, or unmap_mapping_folio()
						 * finds two mappings instead of one. */

---

truncate_inode_folio() zaps the PTEs, thus mapcount = 0.
	shmem folio is locked by both truncate and shmem_fault().

---

I don't see what you're seeing? Note that both map_pages and fault()
take the folio lock (map_pages does a trylock) to exclude against truncate
as well.

-- 
Pedro

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that Race 2 also involves a fork, and plans to simplify the patch by treating both races as variations of the same issue.

The author addressed Pedro Falcato's concern about a potential race condition in the child VMA handling by explaining that the iterator will not revisit the child, thus avoiding the issue.

The author addresses Pedro Falcato's concern that in the copy_page_range() function, the parent PTE is zapped after a child PTE has been installed, leading to a bug. The author explains that at this point, the unmap_mapping_folio function only processed the child VMA and the parent PTE had not yet been zapped.

Author explained that the race is between truncate's unmap walk and fork's copy_page_range, which doesn't take the folio lock, and suggested preventing fork-inserted PTEs from existing instead of making copy_page_range aware of truncation.
review_comments.raw_body:
after looking at your comments below i realized race 2 actually requires
the fork as well, which means they're both essentially variations of the
same race, so hopefully i can simplify the change log.

---

^^^ child VMA done, iterator moves on
		        it will not re-visit the child.

---

At this point, unmap_mapping_folio only processed the child VMA
(no PTE, skip). The parent PTE *has not* been zapped.

copy_page_range() acquires src_ptl (parent) and reads a present PTE,
and boom copies it to child.

When it reaches the parent VMA next, it zaps the parent PTE,
but the child PTE (just installed) survives.

---

The folio lock serializes map_pages/fault against truncate - but the
race isn't between those two. It's between truncate's unmap walk and
fork's copy_page_range - and copy_page_range doesn't take folio lock.

The easiest way to deal with this is to prevent these fork-inserted PTEs
from existing rather than try to make copy_page_range aware of
truncation (it already holds the PTL when it finds the PTE, so you can't
take the folio lock unless you drop/reacquire the PTL).

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Pedro Falcato
review_comments.summary:
Pedro Falcato questioned whether a child-parent relationship can occur when traversing the i_mmap tree, citing the vma_interval_tree_insert_after() function's behavior and its in-order traversal guarantee.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato noted that the races described in the patch are actually not broken, as the parent and child processes hold the corresponding PTE locks in order of traversal, preventing re-installation of PTEs for a folio being removed from page cache. He suggested adding protection against concurrent rmap by unlocking i_mmap_write before calling copy_page_range.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Matthew Wilcox NACKed the patch, citing that ->map_pages() is called under RCU lock and must not block; he suggested that if a page cannot be reached without blocking, the filesystem should skip it and use set_pte_range() to setup the page table entry.
review_comments.raw_body:
NACK.

->map_pages() is called when VM asks to map easy accessible pages.
Filesystem should find and map pages associated with offsets from "start_pgoff"
till "end_pgoff". ->map_pages() is called with the RCU lock held and must
not block.  If it's not possible to reach a page without blocking,
filesystem should skip it. Filesystem should use set_pte_range() to setup
page table entry. Pointer to entry associated with the page is passed in
"pte" field in vm_fault structure. Pointers to entries for other offsets
should be calculated relative to "pte".

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges Pedro Falcato's feedback about the patch potentially introducing contention issues on unmaps and agrees to investigate further.

Author acknowledges Pedro's concern about the patch not addressing all possible races and expresses a willingness to consider additional fixes.
review_comments.raw_body:
hm, i think you're right, i have this inverted.

But this patch objectively fixed my issue, I no longer see this BUG(),
I don't get softlocks, and I don't get the guest corruption I was seeing
previously. It could simply be that the contention added makes the race
less likely.

Let me dig into this and just smoke test your suggestion - but I think
your patch would cause some contention issues on unmaps.

It's been difficult to generate a reproducer for this without running
hundreds of VMs, whatever race is going on here is extremely narrow.

---

Me too, but i never rule anything out.

~Gregory

review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that the issue is not just a scoping problem and agrees to use trylock/fallback to fault if the invalidate lock is needed, but needs to test further.

we take the rcu_read_lock shortly after the call anyway, but I see.

If the invalidate lock ends up being needed then i could leave rcu

But I need to test a few things, nothing else protects filemap_map_pages

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] nfs: fix utimensat() for atime with delegated timestamps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS client where delegated timestamps don't update the ctime when an atime-only change is made via utimensat(). The fix involves issuing an RPC to update the ctime alongside the atime. This addresses a problem introduced by a previous commit that further fixed attribute delegation and mtime changes.

> xfstest generic/221 is failing with delegated timestamps enabled.  When
> the client holds a WRITE_ATTRS_DELEG delegation, and a userland process
> does a utimensat() for only the atime, the ctime is not properly
> updated. The problem is that the client tries to cache the atime update,
> but there is no mtime update, so the delegated attribute update never
> updates the ctime.
> 
> Delegated timestamps don't have a mechanism to update the ctime in
> accordance with atime-only changes due to utimensat() and the like.
> Change the client to issue an RPC in this case, so that the ctime gets
> properly updated alongside the atime.
> 
> Fixes: 40f45ab3814f ("NFS: Further fixes to attribute delegation a/mtime changes")
> Reported-by: Olga Kornievskaia <aglo@umich.edu>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfs/inode.c | 9 +--------
>  1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 8 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/nfs/inode.c b/fs/nfs/inode.c
> index 4786343eeee0f874aa1f31ace2f35fdcb83fc7a6..3a5bba7e3c92d4d4fcd65234cd2f10e56f78dee0 100644
> --- a/fs/nfs/inode.c
> +++ b/fs/nfs/inode.c
> @@ -757,14 +757,7 @@ nfs_setattr(struct mnt_idmap *idmap, struct dentry *dentry,
>  	} else if (nfs_have_delegated_atime(inode) &&
>  		   attr->ia_valid & ATTR_ATIME &&
>  		   !(attr->ia_valid & ATTR_MTIME)) {
> -		if (attr->ia_valid & ATTR_ATIME_SET) {
> -			if (uid_eq(task_uid, owner_uid)) {
> -				spin_lock(&inode->i_lock);
> -				nfs_set_timestamps_to_ts(inode, attr);
> -				spin_unlock(&inode->i_lock);
> -				attr->ia_valid &= ~(ATTR_ATIME|ATTR_ATIME_SET);
> -			}
> -		} else {

This probably deserves a comment. How about something like:

		/*
		 * An atime-only update via an explicit setattr (e.g.: utimensat() and
		 * the like) requires updating the ctime as well. Delegated timestamps
		 * don't have a mechanism for updating the ctime with a delegated
		 * atime-only update, so an RPC must be issued.
		 */

> +		if (!(attr->ia_valid & ATTR_ATIME_SET)) {
>  			nfs_update_delegated_atime(inode);
>  			attr->ia_valid &= ~ATTR_ATIME;
>  		}

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the inode's ctime (last modification time) after a removexattr operation on an NFS file system. The problem it solves is that the stat() function does not trigger a GETATTR request when delegated timestamps are used, resulting in an incorrect cached ctime value. To fix this, the patch adds a trailing GETATTR to the removexattr compound, similar to how setxattr works. This ensures that the inode's ctime is updated correctly after removing an extended attribute.

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Olga Kornievskaia noted that the current approach relies on the server updating change_attr on operations that may not necessarily happen, and proposed an alternative fix to update the inode's mtime when delegated attributes are present.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed concerns about the proposed patch's approach to updating inode ctime after removexattr operation, explaining that doing it this way allows the server to have the most up-to-date version of mtime and ctime, and potentially avoids unnecessary SETATTR operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
What's the advantage of doing it this way?

You just sent a REMOVEXATTR operation to the server that will change
the mtime there. The server has the most up-to-date version of the
mtime and ctime at that point.

It's certainly possible that the REMOVEXATTR is the only change that
occurred. With what I'm proposing, we don't even need to do a SETATTR
at all if nothing else changed. With your version, you would.

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Olga Kornievskaia questioned whether the server should update its mtime/ctime on operations like WRITE, CLONE, and COPY, and wondered if some implementations might not follow this behavior for REMOVEXATTR as well. She suggested that the patch relies on receiving an updated value from the server.
sentiment_signals: request for clarification in the spec
review_comments.raw_body:
In presence of delegated attributes, Is the server required to update
its mtime/ctime on an operation? As I mentioned, the linux server does
not update its ctime/mtime for WRITE, CLONE, COPY. Is possible that
some implementations might be different and also do not update the
ctime/mtime on REMOVEXATTR? Therefore I was suggesting that the patch
relies on the fact that it would receive an updated value. Of course
perhaps all implementations are done the same as the linux server and
my point is moot. I didn't see anything in the spec that clarifies
what the server supposed to do (and client rely on).

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that delegated timestamps may not cover SETATTR style operations and asks for clarification on server requirements, specifically regarding disabling timestamp updates for such operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
(cc'ing Tom)

That is a very good point.

My interpretation was that delegated timestamps generally covered
writes, but SETATTR style operations that do anything beyond only
changing the mtime can't be cached.

We probably need some delstid spec clarification: for what operations
is the server required to disable timestamp updates when a write
delegation is outstanding?

In the case of nfsd, we disable timestamp updates for WRITE/COPY/CLONE
but not SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.

How does the Hammerspace anvil behave? Does it disable c/mtime updates
for writes when there is an outstanding timestamp delegation like we're
doing in nfsd? If so, does it do the same for
SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR operations as well?

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Thomas Haynes noted that the patch's approach to updating inode ctime after removexattr operation does not align with the delegated timestamp model described in RFC8881, where the server should query the client via CB_GETATTR to return updated timestamps while the delegation is outstanding. He suggested re-evaluating the semantics for metadata-style ops like CLONE/COPY/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR and considering whether delegated timestamps follow the same attribute-authority model as delegated size.
review_comments.raw_body:
Jeff,

I think the right way to look at this is closer to how size is
handled under delegation in RFC8881, rather than as a per-op rule.

In our implementation, because we are acting as an MDS and data I/O
goes to DSes, we already treat size as effectively delegated when
a write layout is outstanding. The MDS does not maintain authoritative
size locally in that case. We may refresh size/timestamps internally
(e.g., on GETATTR by querying DSes), but we don\u2019t treat that as
overriding the delegated authority.

For timestamps, our behavior is effectively the same model. When
the client holds the relevant delegation, the server does not
consider itself authoritative for ctime/mtime. If current values
are needed, we can obtain them from the client (e.g., via CB_GETATTR),
and the client must present the delegation stateid to demonstrate
that authority. So the authority follows the delegation, not the
specific operation.

That said, I don\u2019t think we\u2019ve fully resolved the semantics for all
metadata-style ops either. WRITE and SETATTR are clear in our model,
but for things like CLONE/COPY/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR, we\u2019ve likely
been relying on assumptions rather than a fully consistent rule.
I.e., CLONE and COPY we just pass through to the DS and we don't
implement SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.

So the spec question, as I see it, is not whether REMOVEXATTR (or
any particular op) should update ctime/mtime, but whether delegated
timestamps are meant to follow the same attribute-authority model
as delegated size in RFC8881. If so, then we expect that the server
should query the client via CB_GETATTR to return updated ctime/mtime
after such operations while the delegation is outstanding.

Thanks,
Tom

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Olga Kornievskaia questioned whether the server should query the client for attribute updates while the client holds an attribute delegation, specifically in cases where a CLONE operation is performed with a GETATTR attached.
review_comments.raw_body:
What happens when the client holding the attribute delegation (it's
the authority) is doing the query? Is it the server's responsibility
to query the client before replying. Example, client sends a CLONE
operation which has a GETATTR attached. (1) is the server supposed to
issue a CB_GETATTR before replying to the compound? (2) is the client
not supposed to send any GETATTRs while holding an attribute
delegation? CLONE is a modifying operation but client hasn't done any
actual modifications to the opened file so a CB_GETATTR would return
that file hasn't been modified. Is the server then not going to
express that the file has indeed been modified when replying to
GETATTR?

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that Hammerspace server may send CB_GETATTR before responding to getattr, and suggested implementing a shortcut for SETATTR in the same compound as a potential optimization

Reviewer Thomas Haynes noted that the server needs to query the values of the attributes before sending them, as it cannot assume the client wants to know what the server thinks.
review_comments.raw_body:
The Hammerspace server on a getattr checks to see if there is
a delegated stateid (and whether it is the client making the request
or not). If it is and the GETATTR asks for FATTR4_SIZE, FATTR4_TIME_MODIFY,
or FATTR4_TIME_METADATA, then it will send a CB_GETATTR before
responding to the client.

While it does not do so, if the client sent in a SETATTR in the
same compound we could short circuit that. Think a sort of WCC.

---

The server could argue that the client wants to know what it thinks.
But that isn't the argeement. The server has to query those values
before sending them on.

review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about timestamp updates during delegated writes and proposed clarifying the delstid spec to address this issue, indicating that further revision may be needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
(cc'ing Tom)

That is a very good point.

My interpretation was that delegated timestamps generally covered
writes, but SETATTR style operations that do anything beyond only
changing the mtime can't be cached.

We probably need some delstid spec clarification: for what operations
is the server required to disable timestamp updates when a write
delegation is outstanding?

In the case of nfsd, we disable timestamp updates for WRITE/COPY/CLONE
but not SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.

How does the Hammerspace anvil behave? Does it disable c/mtime updates
for writes when there is an outstanding timestamp delegation like we're
doing in nfsd? If so, does it do the same for
SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR operations as well?

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Thomas Haynes suggested that the patch's approach to handling timestamp updates under delegation should be aligned with how size is handled in RFC8881, where authority follows the delegation rather than specific operations. He noted that the current implementation treats size as delegated when a write layout is outstanding and proposed that timestamps should follow the same model.
review_comments.raw_body:
Jeff,

I think the right way to look at this is closer to how size is
handled under delegation in RFC8881, rather than as a per-op rule.

In our implementation, because we are acting as an MDS and data I/O
goes to DSes, we already treat size as effectively delegated when
a write layout is outstanding. The MDS does not maintain authoritative
size locally in that case. We may refresh size/timestamps internally
(e.g., on GETATTR by querying DSes), but we don\u2019t treat that as
overriding the delegated authority.

For timestamps, our behavior is effectively the same model. When
the client holds the relevant delegation, the server does not
consider itself authoritative for ctime/mtime. If current values
are needed, we can obtain them from the client (e.g., via CB_GETATTR),
and the client must present the delegation stateid to demonstrate
that authority. So the authority follows the delegation, not the
specific operation.

That said, I don\u2019t think we\u2019ve fully resolved the semantics for all
metadata-style ops either. WRITE and SETATTR are clear in our model,
but for things like CLONE/COPY/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR, we\u2019ve likely
been relying on assumptions rather than a fully consistent rule.
I.e., CLONE and COPY we just pass through to the DS and we don't
implement SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.

So the spec question, as I see it, is not whether REMOVEXATTR (or
any particular op) should update ctime/mtime, but whether delegated
timestamps are meant to follow the same attribute-authority model
as delegated size in RFC8881. If so, then we expect that the server
should query the client via CB_GETATTR to return updated ctime/mtime
after such operations while the delegation is outstanding.

Thanks,
Tom

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Olga Kornievskaia questioned whether the server should query the client before responding to a GETATTR request while an attribute delegation is outstanding, citing a CLONE operation as an example where the client hasn't modified the file but still holds the delegation.
review_comments.raw_body:
What happens when the client holding the attribute delegation (it's
the authority) is doing the query? Is it the server's responsibility
to query the client before replying. Example, client sends a CLONE
operation which has a GETATTR attached. (1) is the server supposed to
issue a CB_GETATTR before replying to the compound? (2) is the client
not supposed to send any GETATTRs while holding an attribute
delegation? CLONE is a modifying operation but client hasn't done any
actual modifications to the opened file so a CB_GETATTR would return
that file hasn't been modified. Is the server then not going to
express that the file has indeed been modified when replying to
GETATTR?

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary:
reviewer pointed out that Hammerspace server's behavior for delegated timestamps is different from nfsd, specifically noting that it sends a CB_GETATTR before responding to the client when FATTR4_SIZE, FATTR4_TIME_MODIFY, or FATTR4_TIME_METADATA are requested in a getattr operation, and suggested short-circuiting this by sending a SETATTR in the same compound request

Reviewer Thomas Haynes pointed out that the server should not send updated timestamps for SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR operations when a write delegation is outstanding, as it may query values before sending them on
sentiment_signals: requested clarification of delegated timestamp spec
questioned consistency with Hammerspace anvil behavior
review_comments.raw_body:
The Hammerspace server on a getattr checks to see if there is
a delegated stateid (and whether it is the client making the request
or not). If it is and the GETATTR asks for FATTR4_SIZE, FATTR4_TIME_MODIFY,
or FATTR4_TIME_METADATA, then it will send a CB_GETATTR before
responding to the client.

While it does not do so, if the client sent in a SETATTR in the
same compound we could short circuit that. Think a sort of WCC.

---

The server could argue that the client wants to know what it thinks.
But that isn't the argeement. The server has to query those values
before sending them on.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename io_buffer_register_bvec()/io_buffer_unregister_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames two io_uring APIs: `io_buffer_register_bvec()` and `io_buffer_unregister_bvec()`, to more generic names `io_buffer_register_request()` and `io_buffer_unregister()`. The renaming is done in preparation for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, where the new function names will be used directly. The patch also updates documentation and code accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/5] io_uring/rsrc: split io_buffer_register_request() logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the main initialization logic in io_buffer_register_request() into a helper function called io_kernel_buffer_init(). The new function initializes kernel-populated buffers, which will be reused by fuse io-uring. This is a preparatory patch for supporting kernel-populated buffers and simplifies the code by breaking it down into smaller, more manageable pieces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_buffer_register_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, io_buffer_register_bvec(), which allows registering an array of bio vectors (bvecs) to the io_uring resource manager. This is a preparatory step for implementing fuse-over-io-uring zero-copy functionality. The function takes in several parameters, including the bvec array, its length, and flags for issue and release operations. It initializes an io_mapped_ubuf structure and copies the provided bvec array into it. If successful, it returns 0; otherwise, it returns a negative error code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename and export IO_IMU_DEST / IO_IMU_SOURCE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames and exports two io_uring flags, IO_IMU_DEST and IO_IMU_SOURCE, to IO_BUF_DEST and IO_BUF_SOURCE respectively. This change is necessary for the io_buffer_register_bvec() path where callers may need a buffer to be both readable and writable. The patch updates several files including include/linux/io_uring_types.h, io_uring/io_uring.c, io_uring/rsrc.c, and io_uring/rsrc.h. It removes the original flags and adds the new ones with the same values. This change allows subsystems to use these flags to specify buffer direction.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_uring_registered_mem_region_get()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, io_uring_registered_mem_region_get(), to the io_uring/rsrc module. The function allows io_uring command handlers to retrieve information about registered memory regions, including the vmapped pointer and size of the region. This is achieved by introducing a new struct, io_uring_mem_region_info, which is returned by the function if a region was successfully registered. If no region was registered, a zeroed struct is returned. The patch provides a way for uring command implementations to directly access pre-registered memory for passing data.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series refactors and extends the io_uring registered buffers infrastructure to allow external subsystems to register pre-existing bvec arrays directly and get information about the ring's registered memory region. The motivation for this change is to make fuse zero-copy possible, and it splits out from a previous larger fuse-over-io_uring series. The patches add new functions to register and unregister bvec buffers, split existing logic into separate functions, rename and export constants, and add a function to get information about the registered memory region. This allows external subsystems to interact with io_uring's registered buffers in a more flexible way.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1 0/1] mm: start background writeback based on per-wb threshold for strictlimit BDIs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1 0/1] mm: start background writeback based on per-wb threshold for strictlimit BDIs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/1] mm: start background writeback based on per-wb threshold for strictlimit BDIs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the balance_dirty_pages function to start background writeback based on a per-writer (per-wb) threshold for strictlimit block devices, such as fuse. This ensures that background writeback is started when the writer's dirty count exceeds its background threshold, even if the global dirty threshold has not been reached yet. The patch addresses a performance issue where background writeback was not being proactively started for strictlimit BDIs, leading to stalls in the throttle loop waiting for dirty pages to be written back. The patch includes a test case that shows a 3-4% improvement in performance for buffered writes using fio on fuse.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/1] mm: reinstate unconditional writeback start in balance_dirty_pages()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reinstates unconditional writeback start in balance_dirty_pages() to prevent performance regressions for strictlimited BDIs and memcg setups. The removal of this logic caused stalls when IO was throttled while waiting for dirty pages to be written back, but no writeback was running. This is particularly problematic for fuse buffered writes, which saw a drop from 1400 MiB/s to 2000 KiB/s performance. The patch adds the unconditional writeback start back in to ensure that writeback is always running when IO needs to be throttled.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is a specific use case that requires this feature. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that rewriting the workingset shrinker's isolation function to use caller-locked variants would be non-trivial and instead opted for a bool flag, citing maintenance burden.
sentiment_signals: trade-off between code complexity and readability
review_comments.raw_body:
The workingset shrinker's isolation function uses unlock_irq() and
cond_resched(). That would be non-trivial to rewrite - pass flags
around; keep irqs disabled for the whole reclaim cycle; break it into
a two-stage process. This sounds like a higher maintenance burden than
the bool here.

I know there is some cost to this distinction, but I actually do find
it useful to know the difference. It's self-documenting context.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, `folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc`, which allocates list_lru heads for folio objects under the control of memory cgroups (memcgs). The function is designed to be lightweight and RCU-safe, allowing it to quickly check if the list_lru heads already exist for a given memcg. If they do not exist, it allocates them. This patch is part of a larger effort to switch the THP shrinker to use list_lru, which requires this helper function to manage folio objects correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation is reclaiming memory. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is consistent with how other major shrinkers use list_lru.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the deferred split shrinker's list_lru allocation is not properly synchronized, and suggested making the code more concise.

The reviewer pointed out that the patch's explanation of the deferred split shrinker's call flow is unclear and suggested a more accurate representation by tracing through the function calls from shrink_slab_memcg() to deferred_split_scan().

The reviewer noted that the deferred split queue uses a per-memcg list, but also sets a bit in shrinker_info->unit->map, which is used to determine the shrink_control in shrink_slab_memcg(), potentially leading to confusion or incorrect behavior.

The reviewer noted that the deferred_split_scan() function walks the same memcg->split_queue regardless of node, which they consider suboptimal.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested exposing deferred_split_lru as a getter or creating a new function folio_memcg_alloc_deferred to encapsulate the allocation of the list lru, allowing callers outside huge_memory.c to access it without having to use the global variable.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested splitting the huge zero folio handling into its own function, but noted it's out of scope for this series and should be addressed in a future patch.

The reviewer questioned whether there's a potential race condition when the shrinker is freed, causing the deferred_split_lru to be accessed by another thread.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested renaming the 'lru' variable to something more descriptive, citing that single-letter variable names are not conventional

Reviewer Lorenzo questioned why the deferred split queue was switched to list_lru for anonymous pages specifically, indicating a lack of understanding of the change and requesting clarification.

Reviewer Lorenzo expressed concern about a hidden RCU lock in the code and suggested refactoring the function, possibly splitting out folio_ref_freeze() bits into a separate helper function.

Reviewer Lorenzo pointed out a minor issue with variable naming conventions, specifically that single-letter variable names can be confusing and suggested that 'l' is particularly problematic due to its similarity in appearance to the digit 1.

The reviewer suggested refactoring the code to avoid duplicated logic in list_lru_del(), specifically by making it reference _deferred_list explicitly, instead of having it hardcoded in multiple places.

The reviewer questioned the need for holding a lock while calling list_lru_add(), suggesting that using a locked_add_variant would be more intuitive and avoiding the need for irqsave/restore.

Reviewer Lorenzo agrees that the partially mapped logic is correct, as folio_try_get() will only fail to add a reference if the folio has already lost the race.

The reviewer suggests adding an irqsave/restore variant of list_lru_add() to handle the repeating pattern of acquiring and releasing locks in interrupt-safe manner.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested moving a stat counter increment operation underneath an if statement to prevent it from being incremented when the operation fails.

The reviewer suggests refactoring the large if-branch in deferred_split_folio() into a separate patch for better bisectability and reviewability.

The reviewer expressed appreciation for the patch's code deletion and thanked the submitter, indicating a positive sentiment.
review_comments.raw_body:
So here it's:

__do_huge_pmd_anonymous_page() / do_huge_zero_wp_pmd()
-> map_anon_folio_pmd_pf()
-> map_anon_folio_pmd_nopf()
-> deferred_split_folio()
-> set_shrinker_bit()

Yeah it makes sense to make the first bit succinct anyway :)

---

Hmm there's no such function is this kind of pseudocode, essentially?

Wouldn't it be clearer to reference:

shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects ->
deferred_split_scan()?

Though I get that it adds verbosity :)

Sorry not (just) being pedantic here, also so I can understand myself :)

---

Ok so overall there's a per-memcg memcg->split_queue, but we set a bit in
memcg->nodeinfo[nid]->shrinker_info->unit[blah]->map for it, and when we
enter shrink_slab_memcg(), we figure out the shrink_control from the
for_each_set_bit() across memcg->...->unit->map?

---

So at this point, regardless of node, we are invoking deferred_split_scan()
and it's the same memcg->split_queue we are walking, regardless of node?

Do let me know if my understanding is correct here!

Hmm that does sound sub-optimal.

---

It might be nice for the sake of avoiding a global to instead expose this
as a getter?

Or actually better, since every caller outside of huge_memory.c that
references this uses folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(), do something like:

int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio, gfp_t gfp);

in mm/huge_memory.c:

/**
 * blah blah blah put on error blah
 */
int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio, gfp_t gfp)
{
	int err;

	err = folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, gfP);
	if (err) {
		folio_put(folio);
		return err;
	}

	return 0;
}

And then the callers can just invoke this, and you can make
deferred_split_lru static in mm/huge_memory.c?

---

It's kind of out of scope for the series, but I hate that the huge zero
folio stuff has an early exit for persistent but if not, falls through to
trying to set that up - it'd be nice to split out the huge zero folio stuff
into another function.

But probably one for a follow up!

---

Presumably no probably-impossible-in-reality race on somebody entering the
shrinker and referencing the deferred_split_lru before the shrinker is freed?

---

Nit, and maybe this is a convention, but hate single letter variable names,
'lru' or something might be nicer?

---

Why anon? (This review is partly me learning about the shrinker, an area
I'm weak on :)

---

Hm don't adore this sort of almost 'hidden' RCU lock here, but this
function is pretty disgusting and needs serious work in general.

And any function that took the RCU lock and list_lru lock/did the unlock
equivalent would be equally horrible so yeah, I guess needs deferring to a
refactor.

OTOH, this could be a good excuse for us to pay down some technical debt
and split out for instance the folio_ref_freeze() bits?

Could we do something like:

	bool frozen;

	...

	dequeue_deferred = folio_test_anon(folio) && old_order > 1;
	frozen = folio_ref_freeze(folio, folio_cache_ref_count(folio) + 1);

	if (dequeue_deferred && frozen) {
		struct list_lru_one *lru;

		rcu_read_lock();
		lru = list_lru_lock(&deferred_split_lru,
				    folio_nid(folio), folio_memcg(folio));
		__list_lru_del(&deferred_split_lru, lru,
			       &folio->_deferred_list, folio_nid(folio));
		if (folio_test_partially_mapped(folio)) {
			folio_clear_partially_mapped(folio);
			mod_mthp_stat(old_order,
				MTHP_STAT_NR_ANON_PARTIALLY_MAPPED, -1);
		}
		list_lru_unlock(lru);
		rcu_read_unlock();
	}

	if (!frozen)
		return -EAGAIN;

	... rest of logic now one indent level lower ...

Or maybe factor that out into a helper function or something?

static void execute_deferred_dequeue(...) { ... }

With this implemented either way you'd be able to get rid of the else block
too.

obviously only valid if you are able to do the freezing earlier?

---

Again nitty thing about single letter var name, OTOH if that's somehow the
convention maybe fine. 'l' is especially problematic as it looks like a 1
in many fonts :)

---

Maybe worth factoring __list_lru_del() into something that explicitly
references &folio->_deferred_list rather than open codingin both places?

---

Do we really need to hold the lock over all the below, but hmm we can't do
an irqsave/restore list_lru_add(), and maybe not worth adding one either,
OK.

Just seems odd to have <lock> <__unlocked_add_variant> <unlock>,
instinctively feels like it should be just <locked_add_variant>.

---

Hmm, in the original code, this comment is associated with the partially
mapped logic, BUT this seems actually correct, because folio_try_get()
because it does folio_ref_add_unless_zero() only fails if the folio lost
the race.

So I think you're more correct right?

---

Hmm this does make me think it'd be nice to have a list_lru_add() variant
for irqsave/restore then, since it's a repeating pattern!

---

Do we want to put this stat counter underneath the below so it's not
incremented on fail?

---

This applies to the above equivalent refactorings, but maybe worth
separating out this:

if (folio) { ... big branch ... } ->
if (!folio) goto next; ... what was big branch ...

Refactoring into a separate patch? Makes it easier to see pertinent logic
changes + helps review/bisectability/fixes/etc. etc.

---

Overall a lovely amount of code deletion here :)

Thanks for doing this, Cheers Lorenzo

Hi Johannes,

Haven't checked every detail yet, but one question here, might be

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Shakeel Butt noted that the irqsave parameter in lock_list_lru() can be removed if __list_lru_walk_one() is modified to use irq_flags instead, as it's reclaim code path and additional cost of irqsave wouldn't matter here.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:19PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> skip_empty is only for the shrinker to abort and skip a list that's
> empty or whose cgroup is being deleted.
> 
> For list additions and deletions, the cgroup hierarchy is walked
> upwards until a valid list_lru head is found, or it will fall back to
> the node list. Acquiring the lock won't fail. Remove the NULL checks
> in those callers.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

> caller, so that the next patch can unify the lock_list_lru() variants.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:23PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> Locking is currently internal to the list_lru API. However, a caller
> might want to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state.
> 
> For example, the THP shrinker uses the lock of its custom LRU to keep
> PG_partially_mapped and vmstats consistent.
> 
> To allow the THP shrinker to switch to list_lru, provide normal and
> irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the
> addition and deletion functions.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

One nit below, other than that:

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

>  
> -static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)
> +static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq,
> +				 unsigned long *irq_flags)
>  {
> -	if (irq)
> +	if (irq_flags)
> +		spin_lock_irqsave(&l->lock, *irq_flags);
> +	else if (irq)

If we move __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags then we can remove the irq
param. It is reclaim code path and I don't think additional cost of irqsave
would matter here.

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:24PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> memcg_list_lru_alloc() is called every time an object that may end up
> on the list_lru is created. It needs to quickly check if the list_lru
> heads for the memcg already exist, and allocate them when they don't.
> 
> Doing this with folio objects is tricky: folio_memcg() is not stable
> and requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. But it's
> desirable to make the existence check lightweight under RCU, and only
> pin the memcg when we need to allocate list_lru heads and may block.
> 
> In preparation for switching the THP shrinker to list_lru, add a
> helper function for allocating list_lru heads coming from a folio.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:24PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> memcg_list_lru_alloc() is called every time an object that may end up
> on the list_lru is created. It needs to quickly check if the list_lru
> heads for the memcg already exist, and allocate them when they don't.
> 
> Doing this with folio objects is tricky: folio_memcg() is not stable
> and requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. But it's
> desirable to make the existence check lightweight under RCU, and only
> pin the memcg when we need to allocate list_lru heads and may block.
> 
> In preparation for switching the THP shrinker to list_lru, add a
> helper function for allocating list_lru heads coming from a folio.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally, as it's per-NUMA node or per-cgroup rather than their intersection, which can lead to large pages being split on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation enters reclaim. review_comments.summary: The reviewer pointed out that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally, as it is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection, leading to splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation enters reclaim. They suggested referencing shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects -> deferred_split_scan() for clarity and proposed exposing deferred_split_lru as a getter or making it static in mm/huge_memory.c.

gave me 27 addresses for this series and I honestly thought that was

So what you're looking at is a (poor) attempt at trimming down the CC
list based on who I had talked to about these patches at the THP
meeting and who I remember had worked on shrinkers & cgroups.

I can CC everybody going forward, not a problem. Personally I don't
mind getting CCd generously, but my understanding is that some people
do...

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 01:51:04PM -0700, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:23PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > Locking is currently internal to the list_lru API. However, a caller
> > might want to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state.
> > 
> > For example, the THP shrinker uses the lock of its custom LRU to keep
> > PG_partially_mapped and vmstats consistent.
> > 
> > To allow the THP shrinker to switch to list_lru, provide normal and
> > irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the
> > addition and deletion functions.
> > 
> > Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> 
> One nit below, other than that:
> 
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> 
> >  
> > -static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)
> > +static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq,
> > +				 unsigned long *irq_flags)
> >  {
> > -	if (irq)
> > +	if (irq_flags)
> > +		spin_lock_irqsave(&l->lock, *irq_flags);
> > +	else if (irq)
> 
> If we move __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags then we can remove the irq
> param. It is reclaim code path and I don't think additional cost of irqsave
> would matter here.

The workingset shrinker's isolation function uses unlock_irq() and
cond_resched(). That would be non-trivial to rewrite - pass flags
around; keep irqs disabled for the whole reclaim cycle; break it into
a two-stage process. This sounds like a higher maintenance burden than
the bool here.

I know there is some cost to this distinction, but I actually do find
it useful to know the difference. It's self-documenting context.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion, as it is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup but not their intersection, leading to potential issues when splitting large pages on other nodes during reclaim. They suggested converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists, streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers.

review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 12:41:06PM +0800, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 06:21:08PM +0800, Baolin Wang wrote:
> > The balance_dirty_pages() won't do the dirty folios throttling on cgroupv1.
> > See commit 9badce000e2c ("cgroup, writeback: don't enable cgroup writeback
> > on traditional hierarchies").
> > 
> > Moreover, after commit 6b0dfabb3555 ("fs: Remove aops->writepage"), we no
> > longer attempt to write back filesystem folios through reclaim.
> > 
> > On large memory systems, the flusher may not be able to write back quickly
> > enough. Consequently, MGLRU will encounter many folios that are already
> > under writeback. Since we cannot reclaim these dirty folios, the system
> > may run out of memory and trigger the OOM killer.
> > 
> > Hence, for cgroup v1, let's throttle reclaim after waking up the flusher,
> > which is similar to commit 81a70c21d917 ("mm/cgroup/reclaim: fix dirty
> > pages throttling on cgroup v1"), to avoid unnecessary OOM.
> 
> This fix for cgroup1 makes sense to me. For cgroup2, MGLRU shares the
> shrink_node() reclaim throttling.
> 

Ahem, I think that throttling is actually broken and so I'm fixing
that, I shared a patch yesterday for that as I saw Baolin is fixing V1:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/acPOn07xah2eh0WU@KASONG-MC4/

Still need about ~3 LOC change after the patch above since I also
need to clean up MGLRU's force reset of PG_reclaim, will post it
tomorrow as part of the V2 of MGLRU's dirty folio handling rework [1].
That series will improve the batch and dirty handling so fixing that
is much easier and cleaner by sharing same routine. Before that
series it could get very messy and cause over aggressive throttling.

Will post tomorrow as my stress test suite is still running
today, it seems all green so far but just in case.

I initially want to post that after that series but Ridong
suggested to just fix that too, and it actually helps to
deduplicate the code, the change is also small.

And right, MGLRU had some issues with dirty flush previously,
Jingxiang and I fixed it:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20241026115714.1437435-1-jingxiangzeng.cas@gmail.com/

But premature OOM is still an problem, not only about dirty
or writeback, but also somehow related to how aging and
protection works. That new series also fixes quite a lot of these,
e.g. the OOM reproducer in cover letter.
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260318-mglru-reclaim-v1-0-2c46f9eb0508@tencent.com/

Still not perfect but no worry as we will solve all of them
(hopefully very soon):
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/CAMgjq7BoekNjg-Ra3C8M7=8=75su38w=HD782T5E_cxyeCeH_g@mail.gmail.com/

My local reproduce using dm_delay and dd can trigger OOM without
the fix I posted above, and no more problem after that.

I guess storage nowadays storage might just be too fast and it
rarely congest the whole memory so no one reported this yet. Of
course it is a real issue. We did see a few suspicious OOM,
rare but might be related.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/percpu, memcontrol: Per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory accounting system to provide per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting. It converts MEMCG_PERCPU_B from a memcg_stat_item to a node_stat_item, allowing for separate tracking of per-node and per-memcg statistics. The patch also exposes mod_memcg_lruvec_state to be used outside of the memcontrol module, simplifying the accounting process. Additionally, it removes unnecessary accounting of an 8-byte objcg pointer per-memcg-lruvec, instead charging the size of pcpu objects directly.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/memory_hotplug: maintain N_NORMAL_MEMORY during hotplug
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch optimizes memory hotplug by removing unnecessary checks and iterations, particularly in hot paths like shrink_memcg. It achieves this by directly checking for the first normal memory added to a node and setting a flag accordingly, eliminating the need for a notifier. Additionally, it simplifies the iteration process by accumulating node_normal_pages and clearing memory directly. The changes aim to improve efficiency without introducing bugs.

review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that clearing N_NORMAL_MEMORY when restoring set side is asymmetric and potentially confusing, suggesting to maintain symmetry by also clearing the clear side

be a bit confusing for others later on.
